---
title: "Homework 6 (ISYE 6501) — Questions 9.1"
output:
  pdf_document:
    latex_engine: xelatex
---
Sorry in advance to my peer reviewers! (ᵕ—ᴗ—) I like to write out my reasoning and can be... wordy... But I will try to be even more brief this time after taking the advice from HW5 peers!  
<!-- ## rmarkdown::render("edX/GTx.ISYE6501/homework/homework6_answers.Rmd") -->  

## Question 9.1 Answer
I used https://www.overleaf.com/learn and AI to help me shape the code for this question, as I am not as experienced with writing R yet.  
I did not copy any specific lines of code, I just used it as a helpful reference for me to better understand how best to write it.   
```{r hw6, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
rm(list = ls())
set.seed(16)

crime <- read.table("C:/Users/mstout/OneDrive - AANP/Documents/Workspace.MAIN/edX/GTx.ISYE6501/data/Homework6_ISYE6501/uscrime.txt",
                    header = TRUE)

X <- crime[, names(crime) != "Crime"]
y <- crime$Crime

pca_model <- prcomp(X, scale. = TRUE)
summary(pca_model)

k <- 5

pc_data <- data.frame(Crime = y, pca_model$x[, 1:k])
pcr_model <- lm(Crime ~ ., data = pc_data)
summary(pcr_model)

beta_pc <- coef(pcr_model)[-1]
intercept_pc <- coef(pcr_model)[1]

loadings <- pca_model$rotation[, 1:k]
scales <- pca_model$scale
centers <- pca_model$center

beta_pc <- as.matrix(beta_pc) # k*1
loadings <- as.matrix(loadings) # p*k

beta_original_scaled <- loadings %*% beta_pc #p*1
beta_original_scaled <- as.vector(beta_original_scaled)

beta_unscaled <- beta_original_scaled / scales

intercept_unscaled <- as.numeric(intercept_pc) - sum((centers / scales) * beta_original_scaled)

beta_unscaled
intercept_unscaled

new_city <- data.frame(
  M = 14.0,
  So = 0,
  Ed = 10.0,
  Po1 = 12.0,
  Po2 = 15.5,
  LF = 0.640,
  M.F = 94.0,
  Pop = 150,
  NW = 1.1,
  U1 = 0.120,
  U2 = 3.6,
  Wealth = 3200,
  Ineq = 20.1,
  Prob = 0.04,
  Time = 39.0
)

new_city <- new_city[, names(beta_unscaled)]

pred_pcr <- intercept_unscaled + as.numeric(as.matrix(new_city) %*% beta_unscaled)
pred_pcr
```

**METHODOLOGY:**  
I began by loading the same crime dataset used in Question 8.2 and separating the predictors from the response variable.  
Since we learned that PCA is sensitive to scale, I used `prcomp` with `scale. = TRUE`, which centers and standardizes all predictors before computing principal components.  
I then examined the proportion of variance explained by each component using `summary(pca_model)` and selected the first k principal components (here, k = 5) to use in the regression model. (This was to retain most of the variance in the preictors while reducing dimensionality and minimizing multicollinearity)  
I then fit a regression model using the first k principal components as predictors, and because the assignment requires expressing the model in terms of the original variables, I converted the principal component regression coefficients back to the original scale.  
This involved multiplying the PC coefficients by the loading matrix, reversing the scaling transformation, and then adjusting the intercept accordingly.  
Finally, I generated a prediction for the specified city using the unscaled coefficients. The predicted crime rate for the city is **1388.926**.  

**ANALYSIS:**  
Using the first five principal components produced a regression model with R-squared = **0.6452**, Adjusted R-squared = **0.6019**, and residual standard error = **244**.  
Compared to my full model in Question 8.2 (R-squared = 0.8031, Adjusted R-squared = 0.7078, RSE = 209.1), this model explains less variance but uses a lower dimensional representation of the predictors.  
The predicted crime rate for the specified city is again **1388.926**. Unlike the full 15-variable model from Question 8.2, this prediction falls within a realistic range of observed crime values in the dataset.  
Overall, the PCA regression sacrifices some fit in exchange for reduced dimensionality and improved stability. Because PCA removes redundancy among correlated predictors, this model is less sensitive to multicollinearity and provides a more controlled structure for prediction.
